The **demography of Sweden** is monitored by the *Statistiska centralbyrån* ([Statistics Sweden](/wiki/Statistics_Sweden "Statistics Sweden")). [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden")'s population was 10,605,500 (31 December 2025), making it the 15th-most populous country in Europe after [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), the 10th-most populous member state of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and the [89th-most populous country in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_dependencies_by_population "List of countries and dependencies by population"). The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") was rated at 1.43 in 2024,[[1]](#cite_note-1) which is far below the [replacement rate](/wiki/Replacement_rate "Replacement rate") of 2.1.

The population exceeded 10 million for the first time on Friday, 20 January 2017.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) The three largest cities are [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg") and [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö"). Sweden's population has become much more ethnically, religiously and linguistically non-Swedish over the past 70 years as a result of immigration. Every fourth (24.9%) resident in the country has a [foreign](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") background and every third (32.3%) has at least one parent born abroad. The most common foreign ancestry is [Finnish](/wiki/Sweden_Finns "Sweden Finns").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Statistics Sweden projects a Swedish population of 12.6 million in 2070.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Population")]

### Cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Cities")]

Main article: [List of cities in Sweden by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Sweden_by_population "List of cities in Sweden by population")

Sweden has 17 cities with a population of over 100,000 people. Most of Sweden's population lives in [Svealand](/wiki/Svealand "Svealand") and [Götaland](/wiki/G%C3%B6taland "Götaland").

### Fertility

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Fertility")]

The **demography of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden")** is monitored by [Statistics Sweden](/wiki/Statistics_Sweden "Statistics Sweden") (SCB).

The 2005 Swedish census showed an increase of 1,488,322 compared to the 1990 census, an average increase of 88,680 annually. During the 1930s, birth rate increased by more than 88128.5 children per year while death rates fell and immigration surged. In the early 2000s, birth rate declined as immigration increased further, with the context of unrest in the Middle East, upholding steady population growth.[[9]](#cite_note-10)[[10]](#cite_note-11)

### Population projections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Population projections")]

In 1950 Sweden had fewer people aged 10–20 with more people ages 20–30 and 0–10. In 2017 the ratio of male to female remains steady at about 50–50. As a whole, the graph broadens with people appearing to live longer. In 2050 it is predicted that all ages will increase from below 300,000 males and females to above 300,000 males and females. With about 50,000 people living to the ages of 90–100. In 2100 the graph is shaped as a rectangle with people of all ages and genders remaining steady. It narrows slightly at the top of the graph with about 250,000/300,000 males and females living to be 90–100 years old.[[11]](#cite_note-12)
Statistics Sweden projects the following population development in Sweden:[[12]](#cite_note-13)

### Urbanisation and population density

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Urbanisation and population density")]

The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") is just over 25 people per km2 (65 per square mile), with 1,437 persons per km2 in localities (continuous settlement with at least 200 inhabitants).[[14]](#cite_note-SCB2020-15),[[15]](#cite_note-SCB2019-16) 87% of the population live in urban areas, which cover 1.5% of the entire land area.[[16]](#cite_note-SCB2018-17) 63% of Swedes are in large urban areas.[[16]](#cite_note-SCB2018-17) The population density is substantially higher in the south than in the north. The capital city Stockholm has a municipal population of about 950,000 (with 1.5 million in the urban area and 2.3 million in the metropolitan area). The second- and third-largest cities are Gothenburg and Malmö. Greater Gothenburg counts just over a million inhabitants and the same goes for the western part of Scania, along the [Öresund](/wiki/%C3%96resund "Öresund"). The [Öresund Region](/wiki/%C3%96resund_Region "Öresund Region"), the Danish-Swedish [cross-border region](/wiki/Cross-border_region "Cross-border region") around the Öresund that Malmö is part of, has a population of 4 million. Outside of major cities, areas with notably higher population density include the agricultural part of Östergötland, the western coast, the area around Lake Mälaren and the agricultural area around Uppsala.

[Norrland](/wiki/Norrland "Norrland"), which covers approximately 60% of the Swedish territory, has a very low population density (below 5 people per square kilometer). The mountains and most of the remote coastal areas are almost unpopulated. Low population density exists also in large parts of western Svealand, as well as southern and central Småland. An area known as *Finnveden*, which is located in the south-west of Småland, and mainly below the 57th parallel, can also be considered as almost empty of people.

## Origin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Origin")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Population_pyramid_of_Sweden_over_time_by_origin.gif/250px-Population_pyramid_of_Sweden_over_time_by_origin.gif)](/wiki/File:Population_pyramid_of_Sweden_over_time_by_origin.gif)

Population pyramid of Sweden over time by origin

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Percentage_born_to_foreign_born_mothers_in_Sweden.svg/250px-Percentage_born_to_foreign_born_mothers_in_Sweden.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Percentage_born_to_foreign_born_mothers_in_Sweden.svg)

Percentage born to foreign born mothers

The majority of the population are ethnic [Swedes](/wiki/Swedish_people "Swedish people"), or people who can trace most of their ethnicity to Sweden going back at least 12 generations. The [Sweden Finns](/wiki/Sweden_Finns "Sweden Finns") are a large [ethnic minority](/wiki/Ethnic_minority "Ethnic minority") comprising approximately 50,000 along the Swedish-Finnish border, and 450,000 first and second-generation immigrated [ethnic Finns](/wiki/Finns#Finnish-speaking_Finns "Finns"), mainly living in the [Mälaren Valley](/wiki/M%C3%A4laren_Valley "Mälaren Valley") region. [Meänkieli Finnish](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli_language "Meänkieli language") has official status in parts of northern Sweden near the Finnish border.

In addition, Sweden's indigenous population groups include the [Sámi people](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people"), who have a history of practicing hunting and gathering and gradually adopting a largely semi-nomadic reindeer herding lifestyle. The Sámi have lived in [Fennoscandia](/wiki/Fennoscandia "Fennoscandia") from at earliest 3,500 years[[17]](#cite_note-Broadbent-18) to at latest around 2,650 years ago,[[18]](#cite_note-19) with evidence of a distinct ethnic identity linked to an early Sámi language diverging from early Finnish by the first millenium BC.[[19]](#cite_note-20) Sámi presence in Scandinavia does not predate Norse/Scandinavian settlement of Scandinavia, as sometimes assumed — the [migration of Germanic-speaking peoples to Southern Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavian_prehistory "Scandinavian prehistory") happened independently and separate from the later Sámi migrations into the northern regions.[[20]](#cite_note-21) However, Sámi presence in those northernmost regions, called [Sápmi](/wiki/S%C3%A1pmi "Sápmi"), does predate the [Viking expansion](/wiki/Viking_expansion "Viking expansion"), the establishment of [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") as a country, and Sweden's colonization of Sápmi during the 1600s.[[21]](#cite_note-22) It is this presence in the northernmost areas of Sweden, predating the Swedish colonization and presence in the Sápmi regions of Sweden, that is the basis for their classification as an indigenous people under [ILO 169](/wiki/ILO_169 "ILO 169"). Today, the [Sámi language](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_language "Sámi language") holds the status of official minority language in the [Norrbotten](/wiki/Norrbotten "Norrbotten"), [Västerbotten](/wiki/V%C3%A4sterbotten "Västerbotten") and [Jämtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland") counties.

In addition to the Sámi, [Tornedalers](/wiki/Tornedalers "Tornedalers"), and Sweden Finns, [Jewish](/wiki/Jews "Jews") and [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") people have national minority status in Sweden.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

There are no official statistics on ethnicity, but according to Statistics Sweden, around two million (19.6%) inhabitants in Sweden are born in another country. Of those, more than half are Swedish citizens.[[23]](#cite_note-24) The most common countries of origin were [Syria](/wiki/Syrians_in_Sweden "Syrians in Sweden") (1.82%), [Finland](/wiki/Sweden_Finns "Sweden Finns") (1.45%), [Iraq](/wiki/Swedish_Iraqis "Swedish Iraqis") (1.41%), [Poland](/wiki/Poles_in_Sweden "Poles in Sweden") (0.91%), [Iran](/wiki/Swedish_Iranians "Swedish Iranians") (0.76%) and [Somalia](/wiki/Somalis_in_Sweden "Somalis in Sweden") (0.67%).[[24]](#cite_note-25) The average age in Sweden is 41.1 years.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

There are at least two studies that forecast future demographic changes in Sweden largely due to immigration and low birth rates. A 2006 study states that "[based upon current data, extrapolated with relevant assumptions] Sweden and the Netherlands would have majority foreign-origin populations by the end of the [21st] century."[[26]](#cite_note-27) A 2018 study concluded that in Sweden by "2065, the share of the native population is [set] to decrease to 49%, the Western population is projected to fall to 63%, and the Muslim population increase to 25%."[[27]](#cite_note-28) Thomas Lindh, at the time head researcher for the Swedish Institute for Futures Studies, claimed in an interview that by the year "2050, more than half of Sweden's population will be immigrants or second-generation immigrants."[[28]](#cite_note-29)

## Migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Migration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Swedish_population_pyramid_by_background.gif/250px-Swedish_population_pyramid_by_background.gif)](/wiki/File:Swedish_population_pyramid_by_background.gif)

Population pyramid segmented by background. Swedish background in color, foreign background in gray

Prior to World War II, emigrants generally outnumbered immigrants. Since then, net migration has been positive with many immigrants coming to Sweden from the 1970s through today.

### Emigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Emigration")]

Between 1820 and 1930, approximately 1.3 million Swedes, a third of the country's population at the time, [emigrated to North America](/wiki/Swedish_emigration_to_North_America "Swedish emigration to North America"), and most of them to the United States. There are more than 4.4 million [Swedish Americans](/wiki/Swedish_Americans "Swedish Americans") according to a 2006 US Census Bureau estimate.[[43]](#cite_note-45) In Canada, the community of [Swedish ancestry](/wiki/Swedish_Canadian "Swedish Canadian") is 330,000 strong.[[44]](#cite_note-46)

### Immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Immigration")]

Main article: [Immigration to Sweden](/wiki/Immigration_to_Sweden "Immigration to Sweden")

The demographic profile of Sweden has altered considerably due to immigration patterns since the 1970s. As of 2020, [Statistics Sweden](/wiki/Statistics_Sweden "Statistics Sweden") reported that around 2,686,040 or 25.9% of the inhabitants of Sweden were from a foreign background: that is, each such person either had been born abroad or had been born in Sweden to two parents who themselves had both been born abroad.[[45]](#cite_note-47) Also taking into account people with only one parent born abroad, this number increases to one third (33.5%).[[46]](#cite_note-48)

Additionally, the birth rate among immigrant women after arriving in Sweden is somewhat higher than among ethnic Swedes.[[47]](#cite_note-49) Taking into account the fact that immigrant women have on average fewer[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] children than Swedish women of comparable age, however, the difference in total birth rate is only 0.1 children more if the woman is foreign born – with the disclaimer that some women may have children not immigrating to and not reported in Sweden, who are thus not included in the statistics.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

#### Historical immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Historical immigration")]

World War II

[Immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") increased markedly with [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Historically, the most numerous of foreign born nationalities are [ethnic Germans](/wiki/Ethnic_Germans "Ethnic Germans") from [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and other Scandinavians from [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway").[[49]](#cite_note-51) In short order, 70,000 [war children](/wiki/Finnish_war_children "Finnish war children") were evacuated from [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), of which 15,000 remained in Sweden. Also, many of Denmark's nearly 7,000 [Jews](/wiki/Jew "Jew") who were evacuated to Sweden decided to remain there.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

A sizeable community from the [Baltic countries](/wiki/Baltic_countries "Baltic countries") ([Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia") and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania")) arrived during the Second World War.[[51]](#cite_note-Integrationsverket-53)

1945 to 1967

During the 1950s and 1960s, the recruitment of [immigrant labour](/wiki/Migrant_worker "Migrant worker") was an important factor of immigration. The Nordic countries signed a [trade agreement](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council") in 1952, establishing a common labour market and free movement across borders. This migration within the Nordic countries, especially from Finland, was essential to create the tax-base required for the expansion of the strong public sector now characteristic of Scandinavia.[[52]](#cite_note-54) but the influx gave rise to an [anti-Finnish sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Finnish_sentiment "Anti-Finnish sentiment") within Sweden and Norway.
This continued until 1967, when the labour market became saturated, and Sweden introduced new immigration controls.

On a smaller scale, Sweden took in [political refugees](/wiki/Political_refugees "Political refugees") from Hungary and the former [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") after their countries were invaded by the Soviet Union in 1956 and 1968, respectively.

#### Contemporary immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Contemporary immigration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Swedish_and_foreign_born_population_pyramid_in_2022.svg/250px-Swedish_and_foreign_born_population_pyramid_in_2022.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Swedish_and_foreign_born_population_pyramid_in_2022.svg)

Swedish and foreign born population pyramid in 2022

Since the early 1970s, immigration to Sweden has been mostly due to refugee migration and family reunification from countries in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America").[[53]](#cite_note-MPI-55)
According to [Eurostat](/wiki/Eurostat "Eurostat"), in 2010, there were 1.33 million foreign-born residents in Sweden, corresponding to 14.3% of the total population. Of these, 859,000 (64.3%) were born outside the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and 477,000 (35.7%) were born in another EU Member State.[[54]](#cite_note-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) By comparison, the Swedish civil registry reports, for 2018, that nearly 1.96 million residents are foreign-born, a 47% increase from 2010. There are 8.27 million Swedish-born residents, giving a total population of 10.23 million, and a 19.1% foreign-born population.[[56]](#cite_note-58) [Malmö](/wiki/Malm%C3%B6 "Malmö"), the third largest city of Sweden and [Skåne](/wiki/Sk%C3%A5ne "Skåne") County as a whole have taken in highest numbers of refugees who reached Sweden, in particular during the [Yugoslav Wars](/wiki/Yugoslav_Wars "Yugoslav Wars") in the 1990s and the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war") in the 2010s.[[57]](#cite_note-59)[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61)[[60]](#cite_note-62)

The first group of [Assyrians/Syriacs](/wiki/Assyrian/Syriac_people "Assyrian/Syriac people") moved to [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") from [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") in 1967. Many of them live in [Södertälje](/wiki/S%C3%B6dert%C3%A4lje "Södertälje") ([Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm")).[[61]](#cite_note-63)[[62]](#cite_note-64) There are also around 40,000 [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") in Sweden.[[63]](#cite_note-65) Some Roma people have long historical roots in Sweden, while others are more recent migrants from elsewhere in Europe.

Immigrants from [Western Asia](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia") have been a rapidly growing share of Sweden's population. According to the government agency [Statistics Sweden](/wiki/Statistics_Sweden "Statistics Sweden"), the number of immigrants born in all of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") (including the Middle East) rose from just 1,000 in 1950 to 295,000 in 2003.[[64]](#cite_note-scb.se-66) Most of those immigrants came from [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon") and [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria"), according to Statistics Sweden.[[64]](#cite_note-scb.se-66)

Immigration of [Iraqis](/wiki/Iraqis_in_Sweden "Iraqis in Sweden") increased dramatically during the [Iraq War](/wiki/Iraq_War "Iraq War"), beginning in 2003. A total of 8,951 Iraqis came to Sweden in 2006, accounting for 45% of the entire Iraqi migration to Europe. By 2007, the community of Iraqis in Sweden numbered above 70,000. In 2008, Sweden introduced tighter rules on asylum seekers.[[65]](#cite_note-Swedentightens-67)

A significant number of [Syrian Christians](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity") have also settled in Sweden. There have also been immigrants from South-Central Asia such as [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") and [India](/wiki/India "India"). Since the [European migrant crisis](/wiki/European_migrant_crisis "European migrant crisis"), Syrians became the second-largest group of foreign-born persons in the Swedish civil registry in 2017 with 158,443 people (after former [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia")).

Note that the table below lists the citizenship the person had when arriving in Sweden, and therefore there are no registered [Eritreans](/wiki/Eritrea "Eritrea"), [Russians](/wiki/Russia "Russia") or [Bosnians](/wiki/Bosnia "Bosnia") from 1990, they were recorded as [Ethiopians](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [Soviets](/wiki/Soviets "Soviets") and [Yugoslavs](/wiki/Yugoslavs "Yugoslavs"). The nationality of Yugoslavs below is therefore people who came to Sweden from the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia before 1991 and people who came from today's Montenegro and Serbia before 2003, then called the [Federal Republic of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Serbia_and_Montenegro "Serbia and Montenegro"). Counting all people who came from Slovenia, Croatia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Serbia, Montenegro, Kosovo, Macedonia, Serbia and Montenegro, the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia and the Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, there were 176,033 people from there in 2018.

## Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Language")]

Main articles: [Swedish language](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") and [Languages of Sweden](/wiki/Languages_of_Sweden "Languages of Sweden")

The [Swedish language](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") is the official and by far the dominating language in Sweden, and is used by the government administration. English is also widely spoken and is taught in public schools.

Since 1999, Sweden has five officially recognised [minority languages](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language"): [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_language "Sámi language"), [Meänkieli](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nkieli "Meänkieli"), [Finnish](/wiki/Finnish_language "Finnish language"), [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language") and [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish_language "Yiddish language").

The [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages"), spoken by about 20,000–30,000 people worldwide,[[67]](#cite_note-69) may be used in [government agencies](/wiki/Government_agencies_in_Sweden "Government agencies in Sweden"), [courts](/wiki/Court "Court"), [preschools](/wiki/Preschool "Preschool") and [nursing homes](/wiki/Nursing_home "Nursing home") in 26 municipalities: [Arjeplog](/wiki/Arjeplog_Municipality "Arjeplog Municipality"), [Arvidsjaur](/wiki/Arvidsjaur_Municipality "Arvidsjaur Municipality"), [Berg](/wiki/Berg_Municipality "Berg Municipality"), [Dorotea](/wiki/Dorotea_Municipality "Dorotea Municipality"), [Gällivare](/wiki/G%C3%A4llivare_Municipality "Gällivare Municipality"), [Härjedalen](/wiki/H%C3%A4rjedalen_Municipality "Härjedalen Municipality"), [Jokkmokk](/wiki/Jokkmokk_Municipality "Jokkmokk Municipality"), [Kiruna](/wiki/Kiruna_Municipality "Kiruna Municipality"), [Krokom](/wiki/Krokom_Municipality "Krokom Municipality"), [Luleå](/wiki/Lule%C3%A5_Municipality "Luleå Municipality"), [Lycksele](/wiki/Lycksele_Municipality "Lycksele Municipality"), [Malå](/wiki/Mal%C3%A5_Municipality "Malå Municipality"), [Sorsele](/wiki/Sorsele_Municipality "Sorsele Municipality"), [Skellefteå](/wiki/Skellefte%C3%A5_Municipality "Skellefteå Municipality"), [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm_Municipality "Stockholm Municipality"), [Storuman](/wiki/Storuman_Municipality "Storuman Municipality"), [Strömsund](/wiki/Str%C3%B6msund_Municipality "Strömsund Municipality"), [Sundsvall](/wiki/Sundsvall_Municipality "Sundsvall Municipality"), [Umeå](/wiki/Ume%C3%A5_Municipality "Umeå Municipality"), [Vilhelmina](/wiki/Vilhelmina_Municipality "Vilhelmina Municipality"), [Vindeln](/wiki/Vindeln_Municipality "Vindeln Municipality"), [Åre](/wiki/%C3%85re_Municipality "Åre Municipality"), [Åsele](/wiki/%C3%85sele_Municipality "Åsele Municipality"), [Älvdalen](/wiki/%C3%84lvdalen_Municipality "Älvdalen Municipality"), [Örnsköldsvik](/wiki/%C3%96rnsk%C3%B6ldsvik_Municipality "Örnsköldsvik Municipality") and [Östersund](/wiki/%C3%96stersund_Municipality "Östersund Municipality").[[68]](#cite_note-70)

Meänkieli-speakers have the same rights as above in the following nine municipalities: Gällivare, [Haparanda](/wiki/Haparanda_Municipality "Haparanda Municipality"), Kiruna, [Pajala](/wiki/Pajala_Municipality "Pajala Municipality"), [Övertorneå](/wiki/%C3%96vertorne%C3%A5_Municipality "Övertorneå Municipality"), [Kalix](/wiki/Kalix_Municipality "Kalix Municipality"), Luleå, Stockholm and Umeå.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

Finnish-speakers have the same rights as above in 66 of Sweden's 290 municipalities.[[70]](#cite_note-72)

During the mid to late 20th and early 21st centuries, immigrant communities brought other languages, such as [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [Serbo-Croatian](/wiki/Serbo-Croatian_language "Serbo-Croatian language"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language") and [Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Neo-Aramaic_languages "Neo-Aramaic languages").[[71]](#cite_note-73)

## Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Religion")]

Main article: [Religion in Sweden](/wiki/Religion_in_Sweden "Religion in Sweden")

The majority (52.1%) of the population belongs to the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden"),[[72]](#cite_note-74) the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") church that was disestablished as a [state church](/wiki/State_church "State church") in 2000. Until 1996, those who had family members in the church automatically became members at birth.[[73]](#cite_note-75) Other Christian denominations in Sweden include the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (see [Catholic Church in Sweden](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Sweden "Catholic Church in Sweden")), several [Orthodox churches](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") in diaspora, [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist"), [Pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism"), Neo-pietistic (*nyevangeliska*) and other evangelical Christian churches (*frikyrkor* = 'free churches'). [Shamanism](/wiki/Shamanism "Shamanism") persisted among the [Sámi people](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people") up until the 18th century, but no longer exists in its traditional form as most Sámi today belong to the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") church.

[Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews") were permitted to practice their religion in five Swedish cities in 1782, and have enjoyed full rights as citizens since 1870. The new Freedom of Religion Bill was passed in 1951, and former obstacles against Non-Lutherans working in schools and hospitals were removed. Further, that bill made it legal to leave any religious denomination, without entering another. There are also many [Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_Sweden "Islam in Sweden"), as well as a number of [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Sweden "Buddhism in Sweden") and [Baháʼís](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") in Sweden, mainly as a result of 20th and 21st century immigration. There is also a small [Zoroastrian](/wiki/Zoroastrian "Zoroastrian") community in Sweden.[[74]](#cite_note-76)

## Homelessness

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Demographics_of_Sweden&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Homelessness")]

This section is an excerpt from [Homelessness in Sweden](/wiki/Homelessness_in_Sweden "Homelessness in Sweden").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Homelessness_in_Sweden&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Homeless_bed.jpg/250px-Homeless_bed.jpg)](/wiki/File:Homeless_bed.jpg)

A homeless person's bed in [Gothenburg](/wiki/Gothenburg "Gothenburg"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), 2013.

[Homelessness in Sweden](/wiki/Homelessness_in_Sweden "Homelessness in Sweden") affects around 27,000 people.[[75]](#cite_note-Homelessness_in_Sweden_2024-social-77)

The Swedish government's response to [homelessness](/wiki/Homelessness "Homelessness") has included commissioning national surveys on homelessness during the last decade that allow for direct comparison between [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") and [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway").[[76]](#cite_note-78) The three countries have very similar definitions of homelessness, with minor variations.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

Some researchers maintain that measures to counteract homelessness in Sweden are largely dependent on a general premise equating homelessness with addiction, mental illness and deviance.[[78]](#cite_note-80) On the other hand, youth homelessness is considered a child protection problem.[[79]](#cite_note-81)
